review.method: hub triage (269 plays, 18 batches) -> targeted deep-dive (15 severe plays) -> scripted enumeration of mechanical artifacts

triage.reason: Andersen's 1827 poem is a satirical undersea journey mocking the world above, but the play softens it into a generic gentle diver-meets-mermaid wonder scene.

Arc covers only the opening of the 1830 tale (omitting the princess, riddles, and resolution that form its bulk), the second trigger trails off vaguely, and the Stakes contain the anglicism 'to en fremmed' for 'til'.
Unverifiable: no confidently known Andersen text matches this title's content, and the shepherd/elf-king pact arc reads as an invented pastoral allegory.
Unverifiable source, and the arc/triggers are so vague (children play, summer comes, love grows) that there is nothing concrete to direct; a single shared 'Leger' position covers both personas.
Details of the early 1831 legend piece are unverifiable, and the prose slips (English process name 'Sinking', anglicism 'to det dybe vand' for 'til').
Faithful and directable; minor blemish is the misspelled piece 'Pengeskrappen' for skæppen med penge.
Notable: near-duplicate premise with sdu002 is historically correct (Doedningen is the 1830 precursor), and this version is the complete, faithful one.

The source is a brief apologue about why fables were invented, but the play invents a forest scene with a 'truth feather', doubles 'Spejl' as both position and piece, and the abstract allegory offers little concrete to direct.
Arc faithfully renders the happy-man's-shirt tale, but the Company omits the prince and princess who drive the entire arc and both triggers, and 'indseelse' is unidiomatic Danish.
Unverifiable: the title matches a minor Andersen devotional piece but the bird-and-dropped-bread plot cannot be confirmed; persona 'Den gamle' also sits oddly against the arc's 'fader'.
Faithful and well-structured, but the Danish Arc contains an untranslated English word ('they') — a machine-translation artifact.
Reasonable compression of an episodic tale, but omits the theology candidate's final episode and the death-wish ending, and simplifies the two fairies' frame.
Very faithful; only minor drift in calling the goblin's snuffbox (snusdaase) a 'troldaeske'.
Faithful including the missing sleeve/swan-wing detail; minor typos ('fremmet' for 'fremmed').

In Andersen's tale the girl plants a jasmine sprig (not a rose) over the head, and the Arc has 'lindebraed' for lindetrae plus an untranslated English 'to'; only two triggers compress a multi-scene story.
Faithful in outline but simplifies the hundred-nights temptation condition and omits Death's closing appearance.

Untranslated English words in the Danish text ('house', 'to dammen') and 'uger' for 'unger'; original punishes only the boy who began the song, names him Peter, and it is the storks who take the name.

Andersen's First Evening of Billedbog uden Billeder is the Hindu girl floating a lamp on the Ganges; this arc instead stages a garret tableau lifted from the book's frame narrative, and the two static triggers are thin to direct.

Andersen's Second Evening is the little girl chasing the hen and chickens in a courtyard ('I only wanted to kiss the hen'); this invented garden-toy scene even imports the frame's young painter into the evening itself.

Andersen's Third Evening tells of the woman on her deathbed in the narrow street whom the moon once saw as a child and bride, not a mother singing a lullaby with a hymnal; the scene is a generic tableau with thin triggers.

Andersen's Fourth Evening recounts a theatre performance in a small town, not a despondent painter inspired in his atelier (the painter is the book's frame listener); arc is an invented moonlight tableau.
Andersen's Fifth Evening looks down on Paris and an old woman in the palace of the July Revolution, not a harbor farewell; also non-words like the position 'Afskedig' and a one-line vague Stakes.
Invented content: Andersen's actual Sixth Evening is the moon over Uppsala's plain and burial mounds musing on carved names and fame, not a poor student seeking truth in a garret.

Real Seventh Evening is the coast road past the Hun's grave where travellers miss nature's poetry, not migrating swans; arc mentions a young artist absent from the Company, and the English 'to' for Danish 'til' smells of machine translation.
Real Eighth Evening has clouds hiding the moon while the narrator muses on Noah's ark, not a chained prisoner in a tower; 'troest to den lidendes' again uses English 'to' for 'til'.
Real Ninth Evening is a picture of Greenland (angekokk, seal hunt, burial); the juggler-family-with-drum scene echoes other Andersen motifs but is not this evening.
The Tenth Evening is best remembered as the old maid vignette, not a poet by a ditch (which loosely recycles the real Seventh Evening's poetry-of-nature theme); 'een stemme' is off-idiom.
Unverifiable against the real Eleventh Evening, and given the batch-wide pattern of invented vignettes fidelity is doubtful; sick-girl-and-sprout is generic Andersen pastiche.
The real Twelfth Evening is the moon over dead Pompeii with visiting singers, not a lonely rich man in a chandeliered hall.
Unverifiable evening, fidelity doubtful given the invented pattern; 'rik paa varme' is Swedish/Norwegian spelling, not Danish 'rig'.
Unverifiable and doubtful; prose stumbles ('pudser sit erobrede minde', the ungrammatical 'de stolte, slag paa volden').
Unverifiable and doubtful; the tutu-clad ballerina alone in an empty theatre reads as modern invention rather than an 1840 Andersen evening.
The real Sixteenth Evening is the Pulcinella vignette (the clown in love with the prima donna), not a desert traveller with a camel.
Unverifiable against the real Seventeenth Evening; widow-at-grave is plausible Andersen mood but likely invented given the batch pattern.
Unverifiable and doubtful; Company lists both 'Baad' as piece and 'Baaden' as place, a redundant/confusing casting.
Unverifiable and doubtful; the 'satirisk' pitch contradicts the wholly sympathetic arc and stakes.
Unverifiable and doubtful; 'hvisker alle spor ud' misspells 'visker ud' (writes 'whispers' for 'wipes'), a real Danish error.
Andersen's 24th evening shows Frankfurt's Judengasse and the old Rothschild mother refusing to leave her humble house, not a girl with a dried rose; trigger also has the English 'in frankfurt'.

The Tyrol setting and painted saints on house walls are genuine Billedbog imagery, but the devout man with a cross appears invented in place of the original's human subject, and the vignette is a static formulaic tableau.
The chimney-sweep scene is right, but the original's point — the boy's first triumphant climb and joyful 'Hurra!' with waving broom — is replaced by a melancholic daily-toil framing.
Unverifiable: cannot confirm the 27th evening depicts a sinking swan over the sea; the tableau is also thin and template-like.
The doll-left-in-a-tree vignette matches a genuine Billedbog evening, but the production is a single static tableau with generic moon-template triggers.
Unverifiable and doubtful: no recollection of any cuckoo-clock-in-Copenhagen evening in Billedbog uden Billeder; the scene smells invented.

The China evening centers on a young temple priest torn by forbidden earthly love while he weeps before the idol; the play strips that conflict into a serene lamp-lighting ritual by a venerable priest.
Vreta cloister church with its buried kings is genuine Billedbog material, but the industrious gravedigger protagonist appears invented and the vignette is formulaic.
Unverifiable: cannot confirm a coachman-at-the-inn evening; the scene is generic enough to be interchangeable with any night vignette.

The Billedbog bear-leader evening turns on the escaped bear joining small children playing soldiers upstairs at the inn; the play omits the children entirely and has the bear playing alone on the square.
Faithful and rich overall, but the frontmatter description 'før Døden henter ham' wrongly implies Death takes Hjalmar, contradicting both the arc and the tale.
Very faithful; only blemish is the typo 'forvisningen to grænsen' (should be 'til') in a trigger.
Faithful core (bronze pig night ride, art awakening, early death) but compresses the tale's middle (Bellissima episode, gallery scenes) and contains the typo 'begrnsning'.
Character names diverge from Andersen's tale, where the narrator and Aphtanides both love Anastasia; 'Anastasius', 'Apoli' and 'Elena' are garbled inventions.
Loose retelling: the nightingale who sings itself to death for the rose and the Northern poet who presses it in his book are replaced by a generic poor harp-singer whose songs carry it over the seas.
Faithful, but minor slips ('i en bondekone stue', unexplained place 'Ruinemarken', Andegaarden/Isen typed as pieces) keep prose and coherence just below top marks.

Heavily compressed: the seven-story journey and its helpers (robber girl, prince and princess, Finn woman, reindeer) collapse into one vague trigger, and the arc has the English-calque typo 'to hendes slot'.

Unverifiable evening assignment: I cannot confirm the prisoner-scratching-a-melody scene is the thirty-first evening of Billedbog uden Billeder (I associate that evening with the chained bear and the children), and the two triggers are thin and static.

Mostly faithful, but the boy's dreamed future life is reframed as an old couple's memory, 'Bedstefar' replaces the old storytelling man, and the arc contains the English-calque typo 'to alderdommens'.
Faithful to the tale's ending (the old Dovre troll takes a bride himself while his sons bolt), though the elf king's daughters, central to the arc, are missing from the Company.
Faithful except the ending sends both flea and grasshopper into foreign war service, where Andersen sends only the flea; the king is also absent from the Company and the princess is typed as a piece.

Faithful arc, but the tyrant's name is garbled (Andersen's 'Gedebukkebeens-Overogundergeneralkrigskommandeersergeant'), and the prose slips into English ('Kineser is faldet ned') plus the misspelling 'Tyrand'.
Faithful and well written; minor blemish is 'Erindring' listed twice in Company (as process and as plan).
English-'to' typo in Stakes ('ophøje enhver ulykke to et tegn') smells of machine processing, and the ending swaps the original eggshell/wagon episode for boys mashing her into anachronistic asphalt.
Story arc is very faithful, but the declared title is not Andersen's canonical 'Den lille Pige med Svovlstikkerne', and the Arc contains the typo 'For to at holde' (for 'For at holde').
The veteran-and-child framing goes beyond Andersen's brief prison/bird/sunbeam sketch and looks invented, and a trigger contains the typo 'ankomst to Kastelsvolden'.
Recognizable but simplified retelling of 'Den gamle Gadeløgte' (omits the stars' gift and wax-candle motif), with the typo 'give nyt liv to det' in Stakes.

Arc genericizes the tale into seasons-and-transience mood, omitting its defining episodes (the fire, the gilded sparrow pecked to death, the roses' afterlife at Thorvaldsen's grave/museum), which also leaves the triggers vague to direct.
Faithful and stageable, but grammatical slips ('En lærdes mand' in the Arc, 'En lærds mands skygge' in the description) mar the Danish.

Stages only the stormy frame with no picture told, whereas the actual Thirty-Second Evening of 'Billedbog uden Billeder' has the moon witness a prisoner's night transfer; the two thin weather triggers give little to direct.
Invents a farewell scene and a promise between moon and painter that is not in the book, which ends abruptly with the final evening's picture rather than any leave-taking frame.
Faithful retelling, though the Stakes reads Andersen's irony about smug contentment half-straight ('finde sand lykke i det nære liv').
Faithful to The Shirt Collar, but a trigger contains the English word 'to' for Danish 'til' ('ankomst to papirmøllen'), a machine-translation slip.
Faithful arc, but the pitch 'Nostalgi' sits oddly against the story's relentless optimism.
Drops Andersen's frame (the polytechnic candidate's galvanic wish-dream and the showman's relieved return to contented puppetry) and inverts the moral into a puppets-demand-freedom fable.
Andersen's 'En Historie' is about the hellfire-preaching pastor whose dead wife's dream-visit teaches him grace; this poet-judged-at-the-market allegory matches nothing in the declared source.

Andersen's 'Svinene' (from I Sverrig) is a travel sketch on Swedish forest pigs opening with Dickens's pig anecdote; the eagle-versus-sow dunghill debate here is an Aesopian fable, not the declared source.
Arc is faithful, but the Danish prose twice uses the English word 'dandelions' and 'to' for 'til' ('kastes dens hovmod to jorden'), a clear machine-translation smell.
Faithful, but the Stakes contain the English 'to' for 'til' ('magt to at hele sjælen').
Mostly faithful, though it darkens the original: Andersen's tourists see living monuments and memories, not 'only ruins', and Denmark is not 'only a memory'.

Genericizes Andersen's patriotic allegory (Denmark as the nest, named swans like the Vikings, Tycho Brahe, Thorvaldsen) into abstract swans-bring-honour beats that give a director little concrete to stage.
Faithful in outline, but 'Erindring' is listed twice in Company (as process and plan), and the framing family with the storytelling old man and the boy is dropped.
Solid seasonal arc, though the Year as a single narrating persona replaces Andersen's personified year-couple aging from spring youth to winter death.
In Andersen's tale the excluded child outside the gate is a poor little girl, not a boy, and the entrepreneurial 'niece' running admissions is an invention.

Heavily compressed: in the original the willow flute is played generations later by a tutor at a manor party (the goose girl never plays it), and the wind's reordering is a passing episode, not a permanent role swap in her lifetime.
Very faithful arc, but the Danish has machine-translation artifacts ('en fad grød' for 'et fad grød', 'lister op to studentens loftkammer' with English 'to' for 'til').
Arc speaks of honningkagefigurer while the company piece is 'Peberkage', and the Stakes sentence 'kun drømmen og døden kan heles for tabet' is ungrammatical Danish.
Prose errors ('Da bælgens sprækkes', the non-word 'viring'), an unexplained 'Urtepotte' piece absent from the arc, and only two triggers that leave the healing arc undirected.
In the original the house of mourning holds a widower grieving at his dead wife's deathbed, not a mother mourning her beloved; the arc also contains the typo 'Met' for 'Men'.

Andersen's tale is about a heavenly plant that grows and is mocked, is torn up by a swineherd, and whose last leaf comforts a melancholy king; the play's leaf-crumbling-under-the-magnifying-glass plot is largely invented, and its two triggers skip the child's moment.

The ending (engagement actually broken, forced labour under the new name) goes beyond Andersen's short dialogue sketch where the rammers keep their name, and 'lade sig ombøde to en livløs ting' is garbled Danish for 'omdøbe til'.

In the original the young sailor dreams of home and wakes in God's peace rather than dying with a watching comrade, so the death plot is added; prose also has the English-leak typo 'tilbage to barndommens varme stue'.

Triggers cover only childhood and departure; the tale's actual resolution (Ib's gold find, Christine's death in poverty, his adoption of her daughter) is blurred into a vague 'vemodig forløsning', and the metaphorical 'Guldhjerte' piece displaces the plot-critical gold treasure.

Andersen's tale is a gallery of named historical martyrs of genius (Socrates, Homer, Columbus, Galileo, Jeanne d'Arc, Tycho Brahe); the play abstracts it to a single allegorical 'Geniet' vs 'Verden', losing the tale's structure and leaving little concrete to stage.
Faithful in substance; minor deviation in that the deathbed vow was given by Sara's father to the dying mother, not sworn by Sara herself.

Keeps only the generic railway-as-pearl-string idea and drops the tale's defining specifics (the six named towns as pearls and the grandmother's slow-journey counterpart), and the boarding/arrival triggers are too thin to direct.
The kernel (bell in Odense Aa, the river man) is right, but the Arc is a single bare sentence, leaving the production underspecified.
Largely faithful (glassworks, balloon, neck as bird cup, unrecognizing old owner); minor slips: the bottle rides the balloon rather than merely witnessing it, and 'Hygge' listed as a place is odd.

Collapses Andersen's four mice and the multi-story frame (three returning travellers each telling her tale) into one traveller, losing the tale's central structure though keeping the wit-wins resolution.

Renames Anton's lost love Molly as 'Marie' and inverts the breakup (in the tale Molly left him, not duty-bound Anton leaving her); a 'Skygge' persona appears in the company but nowhere in the arc; contains the typo 'sua'.
Faithful compression of the five brothers and the brick that opens Heaven's gate, though old Margrethe is reduced to an unnamed 'stakkel'.
Gets the tree's age wrong (356 vs the tale's pointed 365 years), invents a dryad while omitting the mayfly whose one-day life is the tale's core dialogue, and contains the typo 'Metens'.

Invented plot: Andersen's tale is a satire of a new ABC book's pretentious verses mocked by the cock on the old book, not personified letters quarreling over rank and learning cooperation after spilled ink.
Sound compression of Helga's dual nature and Christian redemption, though the storks, central as narrators and rescuers, are absent from the company.
Retells Aesop's hare-and-slow-runner race (hare sleeps, snail wins) instead of Andersen's satire of a prize jury that gives the hare first prize and the snail second for diligence.

Generic seeker-vs-shadow allegory replaces Andersen's actual plot of the wise man in the Tree of the Sun whose four sons fail through their senses while the blind daughter finds the stone through faith.
Faithful and concrete; minor blemishes: English-typo 'to' for 'til' in a trigger, and the redeeming tear in the tale comes from the weeping child grown old and dying.

Core setup (tower watchman, philosophical visits, New Year musings) is right, but the farewell/death ending and the 'we all become tower watchmen' moral are invented; Andersen's sketch ends open, promising further visits.
Faithful overall; minor deviation in having her die in the sand on the beach rather than in the church where Andersen grants her redemption.
Faithful to the tale and its Thorvaldsen allusion; small liberty in placing the poor boy out on the cold street rather than listening at the ajar door.
Arc is a single sentence that merely repeats the description, leaving the staging thin; it also softens the tale's descent into the grave into a generic 'heavenly revelation'.
Very faithful; one lexical slip ('splid' for the pen's nib, which in Danish means discord) gives a faint machine-translation smell.

Keeps the cucumber and the rival cocks, but flattens Andersen's pointed ending (the weathercock snaps off while the yard cock proves actually good for something) into a generic 'both come to nothing' moral.
Omits Sophie and the second marriage that carries Andersen's point, replacing it with an invented artistic redemption at the wife's grave.

Significant distortions: Clara is the Skagen merchant's daughter met in adulthood, she dies in the shipwreck despite Joergen's rescue attempt, and he loses his mind and dies alone in the sand-buried church; the play invents a childhood romance and a shared redemption scene.
Captures the Oersted-brothers homage, but the arc is an abstract panegyric with only two vague triggers, too generic to direct as a live production.
Good fidelity to the Ole sequel including Louis XI and death's moving-van omnibus; the 'Taender' piece and its mention in the stakes are an oddity not clearly grounded in the tale.
Faithful frame, though the company abstracts the twelve month-characters into a single generic 'Rejsende' persona, thinning what the tale individuates.
Inverts the satire's point: Andersen's beetle returns unrepentant, concluding the horse got golden shoes for the beetle's sake, whereas the play has him humbled and taught his rightful place.
The original is a speculative essay wondering when and how the new century's Muse will appear; the concrete descent-journey-spark-handover plot with a specific poet is invented.

Notable: arc is a faithful compression of the novella (drowning is on the wedding eve, a minor shift), but the company piece 'Iffel' is opaque and does not obviously map to anything in the story (eaglet? Babette?).

Notable: faithful (statue sunk in the dry well, monastery, unearthing at a nun's grave), though the recurring coinage 'gendøbes/gendøbelsen' is idiosyncratic Danish and the original's centuries become 'mange år'.

The original is anchored in Marbach and Schiller — the bell rings at the poor boy's birth and is finally melted into the bronze of the Schiller statue — but the play drops the recasting climax, inverts the chronology into a dug-up bell inspiring a generic poet-child, covers the arc with only two triggers, and has a grammatical slip ('forgængelighed mod overfor').
Arc is faithful, but the Stakes contain the English word 'to' for Danish 'til' ('vender hjem to dem'), a machine-translation artifact that recurs across this batch.

Notable: broadly faithful, but it omits the original's pun-driven frame (the flower sent to 'gjække' the sweetheart) and the Ambrosius Stub poetry-book identification, flattening the story to generic nostalgia.

Very faithful (riveted lid, degradation to flowerpot, broken up for the bulb), but the Stakes contain the English 'to' for 'til' ('give liv to noget andet'); also the original's ironic 'fineste hånd' becomes a 'klodset hånd'.

The source is a non-narrative prose poem, and the play stays correspondingly abstract: only two vague triggers, an arc of pure generalities ('sangens og vækningens processer', 'historiens vinge'), giving a director almost nothing concrete to stage.

Notable: core events match (self-important mill, fire, rebuilding), but the heavy hubris-and-humility moral and the 'sun and moon for eyes' detail are interpretive additions to Andersen's gentler, already-pious mill.

Arc captures the drummer's son, the rallying drumbeat, and the musical 'golden treasure', though it drops the red-hair motif behind the title; a trigger contains the English 'to' for 'til' ('hjemkomst to moderen').
Faithful to the sign-swapping satire though the grandfather frame story is dropped; one typo ('klamrer os to' for 'til') in the Stakes.
Georg becomes a painter instead of Andersen's architect, and the rescued saddle and masquerade-ball reunion are inventions or distortions of the tale's fire and ball scenes.
Rich, concrete and largely faithful (surdej, kanestøvler, Sivertsen, rullepølsemad, Agent Fab); the locked-in-the-loge doomsday vision slightly dramatizes the tale's fire-panic episode.
Captures the jewel-in-the-head longing and bucket escape but compresses the toad's long journey; corrupted word 'sua' in the Arc is a small blemish.
Faithful to the legend and dike-building, though it frames the ending as man-versus-sea combat rather than Andersen's gentle twist that Glænø 'vanishes' by joining the mainland.
Andersen's piece is a brief humorous dialogue about the conceited aphids; the ladybird massacre and near-extinction drama that carries this arc is invented.

Frontmatter description pits the madam's porridge against 'the gardener's verses' while the Arc correctly has the madam herself writing the verses, and the listed Gartner persona never appears in Arc or Triggers.

Andersen's satirical piece about the stork myth and the brothers' comic ambitions is replaced by a generic 'three souls set out on life journeys' arc with none of the tale's actual content, and the triggers stay equally vague.
Correctly centers the scrapbook frame and the tran-lamp versus gaslight theme, but reduces the tale's grand sweep of Copenhagen history to a vague gesture.
Inverts the source: in Andersen the days can only feast ON leap day, their one free day, whereas here Skuddag crashes and disturbs the party, and the who-is-most-important contest is invented.

Invents a Faustian devil's pact absent from Andersen (the dryad's wish is granted by fate at the cost of her life) and omits the tree's transplantation to Paris; 'en enkelt dia' is a garbled word for 'dag'.

Untranslated English fragments ('Rags bliver grebet', 'Here gennemgår de') and 'fører to en heftig disput' give the Danish a clear machine-translation smell despite a faithful Danish-Norwegian rag satire.

Keeps William, the card castle and the fiery ending, but the clean-hands moralizing sermon is doubtful and the four knaves' tales of their kings and queens, the substance of Andersen's piece, are missing.

Faithful core (storm fells pear tree, turner's umbrella fitting brings fortune) but the tale's turned wooden pears are flattened into generic 'pinde'; piece filename 'paeretaer' is a garbled transliteration of paeretrae.
Keeps only the wind-interrupts-sunshine frame and drops the embedded sunbeam stories (swan and feather vignettes) that are the tale's substance, leaving triggers abstract and hard to direct.

Ending is inverted: in Andersen's satire the young man never regains poetry even with the wise woman's glasses and is told to become a critic instead, yet the arc has him happily inspired, clashing with the play's own 'Kritisk' and 'Satirisk' entries.

The donkey never eats the thistle in the tale (it is tethered and cannot reach it), and the pivotal Scottish young lady picking the thistle as Scotland's emblem, sealing the engagement, is blurred into a generic compassionate girl.

English contamination in the Stakes ('forfalde to aske' for 'til aske') smells machine-generated, only two thin triggers, and the title-bearing point that Grethe is Marie Grubbe's descendant (her 'family') is omitted.
Spirit is right (godfather's 'life is the loveliest fairy tale'), but the two triggers and the 'deep conversation about aging' are abstract and give a director little concrete action to stage.
Generational conflict and the west-coast rescue-rocket episode are genuine, though Frederik's America voyage and the clinching telegraph message are compressed out of the summary.

Invents a single narrator-meets-Aamanden frame for what is actually Andersen's collection of retold Danish legends, and the prose carries an English-'to' slip ('soeger to indsamle') plus the muddled phrase 'under den historiske klang'.

The verse's defining refrain device, asking the Amager market-wife to vouch for the story, is absent from the arc, and the soup-pot demise elaborates beyond what the nonsense poem verifiably contains.
Faithful child-versus-Tante-Malle frame around the doll song; minor blemish is 'Leg' appearing twice as both process and plan.
Very faithful to the Larsen tale, but the trigger line 'gaesternes ros to de frembragte frugter' uses English 'to' for 'til', a machine-translation smell, and 'Raegetraeer' garbles raagetraeer.
In Andersen's tale Rasmus actually goes out into the wide world as a journeyman and returns broken; the play inverts this into him fearing the world and choosing passivity at home.

In Portnoeglen the kammerraad is already married and Lotte-Lene is a young protegee whose luck the key foretells (failed stage career, then a good match of her own); the play invents a marriage between the kammerraad and Lene.

The student narrator is misnamed Rasmussen (in the tale Brygger Rasmussen is Aunt Mille's old suitor, not the student) and the toothache figure is renamed Madam Tandpine, though the core pact of pain versus poetry is faithful.
Notable only: frontmatter description typo 'luftslipper' for 'luftskipper', and 'Flugt' is duplicated as both process and plan.

Unverifiable against the obscure posthumous sketch 'Qvaek' (the boy-stoning-a-toad scene rather suggests Skrubtudsen); grammar slip 'begynder at kaster', a templated 'moon shines satirically' line, and only two thin static beats.

Unverifiable against the posthumous 'Skriveren' (which I recall as satire about a copyist with authorial pretensions, not a solemn preserver of memory); typo 'uregistrerde', a templated moon line, an ink persona the arc never uses, and a static all-night-writing scene with no dramatic turn.

Stages only the frame of Billedbog uden Billeder (moon visiting the lonely artist) and invents the moon rising 'for the first time'; the actual First Evening is the moon's tale of the Hindu girl by the Ganges, which is absent.
Unverifiable against the obscure piece 'Man siger -!'; the arc is a generic rumor-satire with an unnamed 'concerned citizen' and vague whispering-crowd beats, plus another templated moon line.
Unverifiable against the early posthumous sketch (details of the woman sharing her last bread cannot be confirmed); internally consistent but the drama is minimal, and the templated moon line recurs.

Core of the monk-and-heavenly-bird legend matches Andersen's posthumous 'Urbanus', but the prose has an English-interference typo 'porten to evigheden' and the templated 'moon shines religiously' line.

Andersen's posthumous sketch is about the potato's generations-long journey from mockery and mishandling to recognition; the play invents a cook-versus-famine plot as the vehicle, keeping the theme but not the actual content.

Unverifiable against the obscure piece 'AEblet'; the planted-seed/dead-girl/surviving-tree arc feels plausible but unconfirmed, the second trigger ('many years pass') is hard to direct, and the templated 'klang' phrase leaks in.

Unverifiable and doubtful: a temperaments allegory with only two of four temperaments (mole/melancholy, merman/phlegm), an orphaned 'Ild' piece with no choleric persona, stilted templated prose, and an abstract 'slowly establish balance' arc with nothing concrete to direct.

The distinctive garden-laid-out-as-a-map-of-Denmark detail matches the memoir sketch, but khai jargon ('realiserer sin plan om dannelse', 'den nostalgiske klang') leaks into the prose and the second trigger ('generations look back') is too vague to stage.
Unverifiable: no work titled 'De blaae Bjerge' with this sun-worship parable plot is known to me in Andersen's oeuvre, so the source attribution is doubtful.
Unverifiable source, and internally contradictory: Hans and Grethe are called 'brothers' though Grethe is a female name, which smells like a hallucinated or garbled reading of the source.

In the novel Eva is revealed to be Otto's lost sister and dies; Otto ends with Louise (absent from the Company), so the arc's romantic resolution 'finder kaerlighed hos Eva' misstates the source's central twist.

The Arc's compound 'Steffen-Kareth' who drowns contradicts the Company, which splits Steffen (blind godfather) and Kareth into two personas; otherwise a faithful digest of Kun en Spillemand incl. the funeral-carriage ending.

Broadly faithful to De to Baronesser, though Dorothea (herself risen from peasant poverty) is flattened into a symbol of old class privilege, and the Arc's meta slip 'Romanen slutter' breaks the play frame.
Faithful Lykke-Peer digest; minor anglicism 'privilege' (for privilegium) is the only prose blemish.

Andersen's juvenile 'Gjenfaerdet ved Palnatokes Grav' (1822) exists, but I cannot verify this Jochum/Stine falling-gravestone plot against it, so fidelity is unverifiable; also the misspelling 'Hjaemsoegning'.
Captures Fodreise's New Year's night, Satanic temptation, Wandering Jew shoemaker and fantastical satire well; some gadget details (hundred-mile boots, glasses) are loose but in the work's spirit.
Unverifiable: I cannot confirm the details of Andersen's grammar satire, and the central conceit is grammatically off (amo governs the accusative, not the dative), which suggests invention.

Arc and first trigger hinge on the seeress Vala, who is missing from the Company, and the ending softens Sigurd Ring's legendary suicide into 'evig sorg'; otherwise true to the saga premise of Andersen's 1822 tragedy.
Andersen's early scene 'Roeverne i Vissenbjerg' exists, but the specific Knud/Ulrik/Henning Aasum abduction plot is unverifiable against it.

The vaudeville's actual love conflict (Ellen torn between two suitors, the tailor and the watchman, with the parterre voting on who wins her) is missing: no suitor personas at all, so a play titled 'Kjaerlighed' has no love interest on the board.
Consistent with Skibet being Andersen's 1831 adaptation of Scribe's quarantine vaudeville La Quarantaine, though specific incidents are hard to verify.
Matches the real subtitle 'En dramatisk Drøm paa Skagens Rev' and the repertoire-satire premise; schoolmaster details plausible but not fully verifiable.
Faithful to Gozzi/Andersen's Il corvo plot with minor slips: the guarded secrets name a 'vampyr' instead of Gozzi's dragon and the bride formula drops the raven-black element.
Arc retells the folk ballad rather than Andersen's 1833 dramatic poem, omitting Hemming and Agnete's mother and replacing Andersen's ending (Agnete's death) with a steadfast eternal-separation close.
Correctly uses Andersen's 'Emmy' Robsart and acknowledges the singspiel's softened non-tragic ending, though the queen-blesses-the-marriage finale is only partly verifiable.

Arc stages the wrong work: Sprogø ice-stranding with agent Blomme/Ingeborg/Theodor is the premise of 'Den Usynlige paa Sprogø', while 'Skilles og mødes' is 'Spanierne i Odense' plus 'Fem og tyve Aar derefter' (25 years, not 20, and no Sprogø).
Unverifiable: 'En rigtig Soldat' is too obscure to confirm the aunt/niece fortress premise, and the near-plotless play-soldier arc is thin to direct.
The invisibility-hoax premise fits Andersen's dramatic jest, but the prose has a grammatical agreement slip ('begynder overbeviste om sin usynlighed') and character names are unverified.
Unverifiable: the 1840 vaudeville monologue exists, but the diligence/homesickness storyline cannot be confirmed beyond title and genre.
Correctly captures the Dorvigny-derived quick-change vehicle and the philosophical barber's parody of Kierkegaard's critique of Andersen.
Setting and heroine match Andersen's 1840 tragedy, but the captured-Moorish-king-reveals-paternity plot and cliff suicide diverge from the play's known ending and are doubtful.
Unverifiable: the declamatory frame linking opera tableaux fits the known benefit piece, but the skeptical-critic conversion plot looks invented.
Faithful to the 1844 Christian II/Dyveke/Sigbrit dream drama, but the prose contains the anglicism 'fysisk confinement', a clear machine-translation smell.

Columbus-on-the-Santa-Maria ending genuinely matches Andersen's Ahasverus fragments, but the arc leaks anachronistic meta-commentary (Columbus performing 'kemiske' reasoning under 'den ørstedske naturoptimisme') into the fiction.

In Andersen's Lykkens Blomst (1845) Henrik is transformed into the poet Johannes Ewald and Prince Buris, not into an invented jailer couple 'Tage and Elna', and phrases like 'under lykkestest processen' smell mechanical.
Arc faithfully tracks the 1847 dramatic poem from Golgotha to the New World, but board-vocabulary leakage ('udfører han sin sjælefred plan') breaks the otherwise good Danish prose.

1848 wartime prologue for the Royal Theatre centenary with Holberg tableaux matches the source; the valkyrie figure and the exact pair of volunteer artists are plausible but not fully verifiable detail.

Excellent match to Andersen's Manzoni-based libretto (Renzo, Lucia, Don Rodrigo, plague, chastity vow), but process names welded into the arc ('under adskillelsesprocessen', 'under redningsprocessen') give a machine seam to the prose.

Spirit-king, diamond statue, truthful girl and Vesterbro balloon ride fit the 1849 eventyrkomedie, but the Stakes praise 'sprogets ... sande værdier' where the arc's theme is truth, a small but real contradiction.

Plot (two roommates married to the same woman, dice game, liberating death-and-inheritance letter) matches the vaudeville, but clunky coinages like 'postbudskab' and meta-leaks ('under vaudevillespøgens muntre toner') weaken the Danish.

Chimney-sweep Christian's dream of riches and the health-over-gold moral fit the 1850 Casino play, but the arc ends in unidiomatic board-speak ('fuldfører hans plan for sand lykke under den glade eventyrkomedie tone').

The Holberg-parody structure of congratulatory visits for a play-as-baby matches Andersen's anonymous 1845 comedy; the specific plagiarism-and-betrothal resolution with Wendel and Christine is plausible but not fully verifiable.
Sick boy, elder tea and Phantasus do echo the 1851 phantasy play, but the arc stays abstract (elements, mole-as-reality allegory, unnamed conflict beats) and is too vague to direct concretely.

Unverifiable: the 1853 one-act opera Nøkken in a Swedish saga setting is real, but the specific Queen-Christine-disguised-as-the-nix plot and character names cannot be confirmed; also one company entry (PehrLaurinForlovelse) is not linked like the others.

Unverifiable: Andersen did write carnival prologues for the Casino artist balls, but the Marschal/Nisse/Genius scenario cannot be checked against the obscure source; note the typo 'Ordenshaandhaefer' in the company list.
Solhvervsgaarden correctly renders the Sonnwendhof of Mosenthal's play that Andersen adapted in 1855, and the Anna/farm-owner/rumor plot fits the source outline.
Faithful to the bridge-treasure-dream folk motif behind Andersen's folkekomedie Paa Langebro (after Musaeus), with concrete stageable triggers.
Birth-vs-merit plot and the poet Kluhd with his tragedy 'Doed og Pine' match Andersen's 1864 lystspil; minor garbled label 'SelvhaabilDigter'.
Consistent with Andersen's five-act Kong Saul opera text (Saul/David jealousy, spear scene, Witch of Endor); the witch's name Zilla not independently verifiable.
Unverifiable: the title is a genuine Andersen stage piece but its actual content (these characters and the proposal plot) cannot be confirmed against the source.
Plausibly reflects Andersen's 1832 intermedium of St. Hans folk life at the holy spring, correctly tied to Holberg's Leonora/Henrik frame.

Andersen's 1846 flop Hr. Rasmussen is obscure so the plot is unverifiable, the Holbergian father 'Jeronimus' smells invented, and the Arc's undecided '(eller Charlottes)' helper is a drafting artifact.
Allegorical prologue source unverifiable, and the Arc's 'museet' (the museum) for 'musen' (the muse) is a machine-translation-grade error.

No verifiable Andersen work 'Truth' matching this generic Truth-vs-Lie allegory, and 'kejsersnit' (caesarean section) used twice for the imperial court is a howler suggesting fabrication or mistranslation.
Tyrolean 'Alpescener' source unverifiable against Andersen's oeuvre, and triggers are act-length summaries (three years of waiting) rather than concrete stage cues.
The vaudeville's actual content is unverifiable, spelling of souffleur/souffleoer wobbles, and triggers are plot summaries rather than directable moments.
Faithfully mirrors Raimund's Der Verschwender (Flottwell, Valentin, Cheristane, Wolf) which Andersen adapted as En Oedeland, but triggers are summary-level rather than concrete stage cues.
Source play unverifiable (robbers tamed by an aria is a stock motif), company names misspell 'Roever' as 'Roeber', and triggers are summaries not cues.

Biographical beats (Siboni audition, Collin/Frederik VI grant, Meisling, Heine, Thorvaldsen, Oersted prophecy, the Grimm snub and reconciliation) are accurate to the autobiography, but the Arc is corrupted by repeated 'to' for 'til', 'sua' for 'sin', and 'mij' for 'mig'.

extra_findings: persona_anastasius.md: Andersen's beloved woman Anastasia has been gender-swapped into the male friend 'Anastasius', while the actual woman is renamed to the invented 'Elena' (persona_elena.md) and Aphtanides becomes 'Apoli' — every character file, both plots, and both plans repeat the garbled trio

pitch_tragisk.md: imposes a wholly tragic tone and 'silent renunciation' ending, whereas Andersen's tale resolves happily (Aphtanides renounces so the narrator weds Anastasia); the emotional arc is inverted, not just renamed

piece_pagtbrev.md: invents a blood-signed pact letter as the load-bearing relic; in Andersen the bond is a church rite before the priest with no written blood-signed document — also 'declared: Graekenland' in place_graekenland.md is ASCII-mangled Danish while the prose uses 'Grækenland'
deepdive.treatment: Restage from the actual tale — restore the narrator, Aphtanides, and Anastasia with the renunciation-into-happy-wedding ending — or retitle as a free adaptation.

extra_findings: All 15 board files (personas, plots, plans, places, pieces, pitch) build the poet-judged-at-market allegory; nothing of Andersen's hellfire pastor, his dead wife, or the dream of grace survives anywhere — only the title matches

position_soegende.md and process_sogning.md: garbled Danish — English 'to' substituted for 'til' ('Nærheden to livets lette glæder', 'tvinger Digteren to at mærke'), and 'Sogning' is a misspelling of 'Søgning' used as the declared name
persona_verden.md: 'Verden' is a many-headed market crowd cast as a single persona with a pointing finger — an unplayable abstraction rather than a performable character
deepdive.treatment: Restage from the actual source (the damnation-preaching pastor taught mercy by his dead wife's dream-visit); the current board shares only the title and must be rebuilt, with the Danish prose cleaned in the process.

extra_findings: pitch_satirisk.md: explicitly attributes the invented eagle-vs-sow fable to 'den spidse, H.C. Andersenske ironi', misrepresenting an original Aesopian invention as Andersen's own satire

process_pludder.md: 'Pludder' means chatter/babble in Danish, not wallowing — the file glosses it as a mud bath ('Wallowing') and plot_striden.md uses 'pludre' for wallowing in mud, a lexical error baked into the play's Company list

plot_stalden.md vs plot_striden.md/play_svinene.md: the eagle sits 'på en bjælke under loftet' inside the sty in one plot but is fixed on the outdoor dunghill on the mark in the play arc, plan_status.md, and the other plot — contradictory staging for a bird whose wings are described as clipped
deepdive.treatment: Either retitle as an original Aesopian fable dropping the Andersen attribution, or restage from the actual 'Svinene' travel sketch in I Sverrig; nothing of the source survives.

extra_findings: plot_erkendelsen.md: English word 'with' dropped into the Danish Stage line ('i studerekammeret with forstoerrelsesglasset')
process_aabenbaring.md: garbled Danish 'ned to jorden' (English 'to' for 'til') in Direction

persona_den_laerde.md and plan_undersoegelse.md say the leaf is examined under a 'mikroskop', contradicting the play/plots/piece where the sole staged instrument is the forstoerrelsesglas (magnifying glass)
deepdive.treatment: Restage from Andersen's actual tale (plant mocked and torn up by the swineherd, last leaf comforting the melancholy king), since the invented angel/child/scholar board shares no scene with the source, or retitle as a free adaptation.

extra_findings: pitch_laererik.md ('hovmod foerer to splittelse') and process_strid.md ('hvisker to bogstaverne'): recurring garbled Danish, English 'to' in place of 'til'
position_sidste.md: non-Danish word in 'vente paa sua tur', and casting C as the 'Sidste' (last) letter contradicts play_abc_bogen.md/piece_abc_bog.md, which state the book runs 'fra A til AA'

plan_orden.md: the Orden plan is owned by A, contradicting the Arc in play_abc_bogen.md where B and the middle letters demand order; A ends up owning both rival plans (Magt and Orden) while B and C own none
deepdive.treatment: Restage from Andersen's actual satire (the cock atop the old ABC book mocking the new book's pretentious verses); the quarrelling personified letters A/B/C are wholly invented and cannot be patched by prose cleanup.

extra_findings: play_hurtigloeberne.md Arc 'laegger sig to at sove' and persona_haren.md 'fra side to side': recurring garbled Danish, English 'to' for 'til'
plot_dommen.md: ungrammatical Danish 'indser sit tabt vaeddeloeb'

piece_pris.md: the prize is called 'den gyldne belonning' (and 'den gyldne pris' in play/plan_sejr), but its Apparent section defines it as a wreath of fresh green laurel leaves with a red silk ribbon - internally contradictory prop
deepdive.treatment: Restage from Andersen's actual jury satire (no race is run: the hare is awarded first prize and the snail second for diligence, mocking prize committees), or explicitly retitle the current board as an Aesop 'tortoise and hare' adaptation.

extra_findings: persona_skygge.md: garbled Danish/English hybrid in Projection — 'som gliding lydløst langs murene' uses the English word 'gliding' mid-Danish sentence

plan_afsloering.md + plan_vildledning.md: both plans have every target pre-checked [x], and they are mutually exclusive — the seeker's plan claims he recognized and rejected the shadow's false offers while the shadow's plan claims it made him abandon his true search; both cannot be complete, and pre-checked targets leave nothing to play
persona_soegende.md: Projection has him carrying 'en gammel skriftrulle' (a scroll) while every other file, including his own Action, gives him the bog as his single defining prop
deepdive.treatment: Restage from the actual source: the wise man in the Tree of the Sun, his four sons failing through their senses, and the blind daughter finding the stone through faith — none of which survives anywhere on this board.

extra_findings: plan_livsvej.md + plan_livskraft.md: both plans use an invalid checkbox marker '- [f]' on their final target (neither [ ] nor [x]), an undefined state the guard/board grammar does not support

plot_rejsen.md (and the play company): only Peiter and Peer own plans; Peter 'følger den slagne vej uden store armbevægelser' with no plan, position-orders aside, making the middle brother undirected and effectively unplayable in the final plot

persona_peter.md: garbled Danish in Projection — 'hvis skikkelser og bevægelser' (plural 'skikkelser' for one boy's figure); pitch_livets_begyndelse.md also breaks the board's own fiction by asserting 'storken ikke bringer børnene'
deepdive.treatment: Keep the genuinely faithful stork-myth frame (pond souls, shooting stars, clear vs muddy water) but restore Andersen's satire and the brothers' actual comic ambitions and fates in place of the invented sentimental birth-order allegory.

extra_findings: plot_paafundet.md: garbled Danish/English hybrid in Action — 'rækker den kloge Kone sine magiske Briller to den unge Digter' uses English 'to' for Danish 'til'

pitch_satirisk.md contradicts the plots: the pitch's Nerve exposes the young man's block as 'ren og skær dovenskab', but plot_paafundet.md, plan_undervisning.md and both processes play the redemption straight, rewarding him with genuine inspiration — the declared satire has no object left

piece_bog.md: internally contradictory prop — Taxonomy calls it 'fuld af tomme sider og hemmelige optegnelser', plot_moedet.md builds tension on 'de tomme sider', yet Apparent/Yearbook describe pages written down by generations; the book also replaces the source's hørerør (ear trumpet) with an invented object
deepdive.treatment: Restage the ending from the source: he never gains the gift of poetry, cannot keep the wise woman's spectacles and ear trumpet, and is told to become a critic and beat the poets instead — or retitle the current version as a free adaptation.

extra_findings: plot_erindringen.md: garbled Danish-English hybrid 'ophøjes to et symbol' (English 'to' for Danish 'til') in the Tension section
plot_naboskabet.md: Stage section mixes English into Danish ('Tidselen stands rank') and misspells 'hegnet' as 'hegdet'
process_vaekst.md: growth is 'Initiated by ... efterårets regn' (autumn rain) though plot_naboskabet stages the thistle already in full bloom at midsummer — internal season contradiction
deepdive.treatment: Restage from the actual tale: the tethered donkey that cannot reach the thistle, and the Scottish young lady who picks the emblem-flower for the buttonhole and seals the engagement, replacing the invented devoured-thistle and pressed-keepsake ending.

extra_findings: pitch_ironisk.md: garbled Danish 'mennesket ofte søer dybe, mystiske svar' ('søer' for 'søger') in the Undertow section
process_snoeren.md: English 'to' for Danish 'til' ('overfører kammerrådens mindste rystelser to genstanden') in the Lever section
piece_viser.md: the 'Viser' piece is defined as 'Den usynlige linje eller bevægelse' — an invisible motion cast as a physical stage piece, unplayably vague as a prop
deepdive.treatment: Restage from the actual source: restore the already-married kammerraad (with the kammerraadinde) and Lotte-Lene as the young protégée whose theatrical luck the key foretells, removing the invented kammerraad-Lene marriage and the superstition-cured-by-wedlock arc.

extra_findings: plot_foreningen.md: even the borrowed reunion motif is off — reunion 'når tyve år er gået', while the declared work's second part is explicitly 'Fem og tyve Aar derefter' (twenty-five years)

plan_hjaelp.md: the antagonist 'agent Blomme' is load-bearing (Orders: 'Afled agent Blommes opmærksomhed') yet has no persona or position file anywhere on the board, so the blocking father cannot be cast
persona_andreas.md: garbled Danish 'Troser vintervejret' for 'Trodser' in the Action section
deepdive.treatment: Restage from the actual 'Skilles og mødes' (Spanierne i Odense plus Fem og tyve Aar derefter), or re-declare the existing Sprogø ice-stranding board under its true source 'Den Usynlige paa Sprogø'.

extra_findings: plot_snoebolden.md: declared 'SnoeboldenPlot' (the snowball) has nothing to do with its content (a gold-temptation finale), and its Action locates 'det svaere faengselsmiljoe' inside place_skov.md (Jaegersborg forest), whose own Withheld section explicitly excludes the prison — a direct place contradiction.

process_forvandling.md vs plan_kaerlighedsvalg.md/plot_tidenskifter.md: Forvandling's Lever includes 'tab af oprindelige minder' (loss of original memories), yet the plan requires the couple to consciously remember their forest life and resist temptation, and plot_tidenskifter has them waking still bearing their inner feelings — the memory-loss mechanic is contradicted by every file that uses it.

play_lykkens_blomst.md: none of Andersen's actual transformation targets (the poet Johannes Ewald, Prince Buris) or Henrik's wish-driven premise appear anywhere; the invented jailer couple 'Tage/Elna' is named in the Arc, persona_henrik.md, persona_johanne.md and process_forvandling.md, so the invention is structural, not a hub typo.
deepdive.treatment: Restage from Andersen's actual 1845 play (Henrik's transformations into Johannes Ewald and Prince Buris), or explicitly retitle as a free adaptation, since the invented Tage/Elna prison-test plot is load-bearing in every board file.

extra_findings: All 15 files build the invented garret tableau (persona_pigen.md, place_gaden.md, plot_udsigt.md, plan_erindring.md, etc.); there is no trace of the Ganges, the Hindu girl, or the floating lamp anywhere — the tableau is lifted from Billedbog uden Billeder's frame narrative (the poor painter in his garret), confirming the series-wide invented-tableau pattern.

persona_maanen.md: garbled Danish gender — the moon is 'den/dens' in Taxonomy and Shadow but 'Det kaster'/'Det skinner' (neuter) in Action and Tell within the same file; the sister file in sdu031 consistently uses 'den'.

plot_natten.md Stage says 'Ingen taler, ingen lytter' while position_fortaeller.md's Drives is to share the moment 'med dem, der lytter i moerket' and process_fortaelling.md promises a 'mundtlig erindring' — the frame's poet-listener is absent, so the narration process has no receiver and the files contradict each other.
deepdive.treatment: Restage from the actual First Evening (the Hindu girl floating her lamp on the Ganges), moving the garret/moon frame material out to a single series-level frame board instead of re-inventing it per evening.

extra_findings: plot_studiet.md Stage: garbled Danish — 'Hvert aandedraet danner a lille taage' (English article 'a' embedded in the Danish sentence); the same file also renders 'den Gammel Bog' ungrammatically.

The entire board (persona_studenten.md, place_kvistkammeret.md, plan_sandhed.md, process_studie.md) stages the poor student's garret with no trace of Uppsala, the plain, the Fyris, or the burial mounds; the content is a near-duplicate of sdu026's frame-narrative tableau (moon at a garret window), demonstrating the invented-tableau pattern pervades the aften series.

place_vinduet.md is typed khai: place while the equivalent window in sdu026 (piece_vindue.md) is typed khai: piece — inconsistent taxonomy for the same object across the series; both plays' plans also ship with all Targets pre-checked [x], leaving nothing playable.
deepdive.treatment: Restage from the actual Sixth Evening (the moon over Uppsala's plain, the river, and the burial mounds); the garret-student tableau belongs, if anywhere, to a shared frame-narrative board, not to this evening.
